raw_transcript:
Doctor: Good afternoon, Mike. It’s been a while. How have you been managing?
Patient: Not great, Dr. Chen. My stomach is a mess. Every time I eat, I get this dull, gnawing pain right in the center of my upper belly. It goes straight through to my back. It makes me not want to eat at all.
Doctor: That pain pattern—epigastric radiating to the back—is very typical for chronic pancreatitis. How are your bowel movements? Have you noticed anything unusual about them?
Patient: This is embarrassing, but they're... they're oily. Often they float, and they're incredibly foul-smelling. I have to flush the toilet twice just to get rid of the residue. It's really gross.
Doctor: Don't be embarrassed; that's actually a vital clinical sign called steatorrhea. It means your pancreas isn't producing enough enzymes to break down the fats in your food. We've had you on Creon, 24,000 units with meals. Are you taking it with every single snack and meal?
Patient: I try to, but I'm still losing weight. I've lost twelve pounds in the last two months. I feel like the Creon isn't doing anything.
Doctor: You're likely under-dosed. 24,000 units is a starting dose, but for someone with your symptoms and weight loss, we need to be much more aggressive. I'm going to triple your dose to 72,000 units with every full meal, and 36,000 with any snacks.
Patient: Seventy-two thousand? That’s a lot of pills.
Doctor: Do you exercise regularly? What kind of activity do you do?
Doctor: How much water are you drinking in a typical day?
Patient: We have a golden retriever. I'm not allergic, thankfully.
Doctor: How about your family? Any significant heart disease or early strokes?
Doctor: Any history of seizures, fainting, or blackouts?
Doctor: Have you noticed any shortness of breath when you're walking up stairs?
Patient: I'm a software engineer, so I sit at a desk most of the day.
Doctor: Any chest tightness or pressure that comes on with exertion?
Patient: Just the typical dry skin I get when the heater is on.
Doctor: How is your appetite generally? Are you able to enjoy your meals?
Doctor: Any history of hearing loss or ringing in your ears?
Patient: I had a kidney stone about ten years ago. One was enough for a lifetime.
Patient: I'm usually okay in the morning, but by two o'clock, I feel like I need a three-hour nap.
Patient: Sometimes if I've been standing a lot, but usually they look normal in the morning.
Doctor: Do you have any history of heart palpitations or a racing heart?
Doctor: Any history of depression or anxiety that required medication?
Doctor: Do you wear glasses or contacts?
Doctor: What kind of work do you do? Is it physically demanding?
Doctor: Do you find yourself eating late at night or skipping meals often?
Doctor: Have you had a recent COVID-19 infection or any lingering symptoms?
Doctor: Do you use any tobacco products, including vaping?
Patient: Only when I have too much caffeine in the morning.
Doctor: Are you having any pain or stiffness in your neck or shoulders?
Doctor: Have you ever had any surgeries on your lungs or heart?
Patient: I usually have a late snack, but I try to eat three meals.
Patient: Occasionally in my left foot if I sit in one position for too long.
Doctor: Have you noticed any changes in your skin, like unusual dryness or new marks?
Patient: My aunt had a clot in her leg after a long flight once.
Patient: The stomach issues have me a bit stressed, naturally, but my mood is generally okay.
Doctor: Any history of glaucoma or cataracts?
Patient: Yes, I got my flu shot last October.
Doctor: Have you had any recent travel outside of the country, perhaps to a place where the water might not be safe?
Patient: I try to if I'm going to be outside for a long time.
Patient: My mother has a slow thyroid, but as far as I know, I'm okay.
Patient: I had it last year, but I felt better after about a week.
Patient: Probably not enough. Maybe three or four glasses. I drink a lot of coffee though.
Doctor: It is, but the goal is to stop the malabsorption. I'm also worried about your long-term nutrition. Have you had any issues with your blood sugar lately?
Patient: I've been feeling a bit more thirsty than usual, and I'm peeing more at night. Why?
Doctor: Chronic pancreatitis can damage the insulin-producing cells in the pancreas too, leading to 'Type 3c' diabetes. I'm going to check your A1c today. I also want to check your levels of fat-soluble vitamins—A, D, E, and K—since you haven't been absorbing fats well.
Patient: Okay. And the pain? Is there anything else for that?
Doctor: We'll try to manage it with the enzyme optimization first, as that sometimes helps the 'feedback loop' that causes pain. If not, we may need to discuss a celiac plexus block, but let's try the higher Creon dose first. Are you still able to stay away from alcohol?
Patient: Three years stone-cold sober, Doctor. I'm never going back there.
Doctor: I’m very proud of you for that. Alcohol is the leading cause of flares, so staying sober is the best thing you can do for your health. I'll see you back in four weeks to check your weight.
Patient: Thank you, Dr. Chen. I'll start the new dose today.
corrected_note: Hand-crafted clinical encounter for Chronic Pancreatitis & Exocrine Insufficiency
